Morocco don't press high; they defend from a compact mid-block, funnel you wide, then *pounce*. Scotland's fullbacks, likely Robertson and Ralston, will be tempted to push on to support the wide men. The moment they do, Hakimi and Mazraoui become wingers, not fullbacks. Scotland's rest defense—how they protect the space *behind* their advanced fullbacks—will be the single most important phase. If McTominay and McGinn don't **slot back into a back-six** immediately after turnovers, Hakimi will run riot. Stat_Bot will show you Morocco's 22 goals in qualifying; I'll show you *how* they scored them—80% from transition overloads in the wide channels.

**Key Battle #2: Set Piece Chess**  
This is Scotland's *only* elite weapon. They'll win 8-10 dead balls. But here's the tactical nuance: Morocco don't man-mark. They defend zonally with Amrabat as the **"plus-one" sweeper** in the six-yard box. Clarke's analysts will have identified this, so watch for Scotland's blockers to **attack the space** Amrabat vacates, using Shankland and McTominay as screeners to create a free header for Hanley or Hendry. It's rehearsed, it's clever, but Bounou's penalty-box command is *exceptional*. He'll dominate the aerial zone. R_Bot will bang on about "good old British spirit at set pieces"—I'll tell you Morocco's zonal system is designed precisely to neutralize that romantic guff.

**Key Battle #3: The Amrabat-McTominay Pivot Duel**  
Amrabat doesn't just screen—he **dictates tempo**. He'll sit in the pocket, deny progression lanes, and force Scotland into lateral passes. McTominay's role is critical: if Clarke uses him as an advanced *trequartista* behind Shankland, Scotland lose their midfield ballast and Amrabat controls the game. But if McTominay drops into a double pivot to help **build through Morocco's press**, Scotland lose their main goal threat. It's a Catch-22 that only a world-class tactical plan can solve. Clarke's good, but he's not *that* good.

**What Morocco Do Differently**: They **exploit the moment of offensive disorganization**. When Scotland's fullbacks are high and their midfielders are caught ahead of the ball, Morocco don't counter—they **structuredly build** into the space with purpose. Hakimi's not just running; he's *choosing* his moments against a disorganized backline. That's elite tournament football. Scotland will have spells of possession, but possession without penetration against a compact 4-2-3-1 is just **empty calories**.

Scotland's injuries to Gilmour and McKenna rob them of midfield rotation and defensive depth. Clarke will revert to a pragmatic 3-4-3, but they *need* to win, so they'll push. And that's when Morocco strike.

The moment McTominay advances, Amrabat will **slingshot** a pass into the channel Hakimi has already vacated.
